nativeRange.habitat: Clear lagoon and seaward reefs; tends to be found in shallow exposed areas, often in small to large aggregations or occasionally solitary.

decorPreferences: highly oxygenated water with strong flow

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on filamentous and turf algae across exposed reef substrates in the wild. In aquaria, generally advised to offer dried marine seaweed (nori) on a veggie clip three or more times per week, alongside spirulina-based prepared foods and vegetable matter. Tends to accept meaty foods such as mysis and brine during community feedings, but an algae-leaning diet is generally considered important for immune health, coloration, and reduced HLLE risk. Frequent small feedings throughout the day better mimic natural grazing behaviour.

summary: An Indo-West Pacific surgeonfish with a dark brown body, bright white cheek patch, and yellow caudal and dorsal markings. Generally considered reef-safe and best kept as the only tang in a large, mature system; tends to be territorial toward similarly shaped tankmates.
careNotes:
The Powder Brown Tang (Acanthurus japonicus) is also sold under the trade names White-Faced Tang and White-Cheek Surgeonfish. It is frequently confused at the retail level with the true 'Powder Brown' of the older trade, Acanthurus nigricans (now more correctly called the Whitecheek Tang) — the two species form a hybridizing complex and have subtly different head and fin patterns. A. japonicus has a more extensive white facial patch extending from the eye to the mouth and brighter yellow pectoral- and caudal-fin markings; A. nigricans has a smaller, more cheek-restricted patch.

Adults tend to reach about 20 cm total length in captivity, with FishBase recording wild maxima up to 23 cm. A mature, established saltwater system of around 500 L (130 gal) or larger is generally advised — this species is an active swimmer that benefits from a long footprint and ample open water above the rockwork. Adding only to systems around a year old or older tends to improve long-term success, as stable water and well-established algal grazing surfaces are commonly considered important.

This species is commonly described as moderately difficult to keep. It tends to be sensitive during acclimation and is reported to be relatively susceptible to ich and Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) — clean, highly oxygenated water and a vitamin-rich, algae-heavy diet are generally advised to mitigate both. Temperature is generally advised around 23–27°C; standard reef parameters apply (SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12).

Temperament is generally peaceful toward unrelated reef fish but tends to be territorial toward other Acanthurus tangs and similarly shaped surgeonfishes. Best kept singly, or — only in very large systems — with multiple tangs added simultaneously as juveniles to diffuse aggression. The species has been reported to hybridize with the closely related Whitecheek Tang (A. nigricans) in the wild.

Like all acanthurids, this species possesses a sharp caudal-peduncle scalpel used for defense; it is not venomous but can inflict lacerations on handlers and tankmates and should be handled with care during transfers.

Source cross-check notes: Adult size — LiveAquaria 8 in (20 cm), FishBase 23 cm TL, Aquarium Specialty 20 cm, Reef Chasers ~20 cm; averaged to 18–23 cm. Temperature — LiveAquaria 72–78°F, AquariumDomain 72–79°F, FishBase wild 24–28°C; averaged to 23–27°C. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria 130 gal (~492 L), Reef Chasers and Aquarium Specialty 125 gal (~473 L), AquariumDomain 90 gal (~340 L); biased toward the larger value and rounded to 500 L given this species' need for open swimming space, stable conditions, and HLLE-prevention margins. Lifespan reported as 10–15 years in optimal conditions.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Acanthurus japonicus has not been documented in available hobby or scientific sources. In the wild it is a broadcast spawner and is part of a species complex (with A. nigricans, A. achilles, and A. leucosternon) in which natural hybridization has been reported. Spawning in home aquaria is considered highly unlikely given the tank volumes required and the absence of reported successes in the hobby literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Powder Brown Tang. Provided adult size (8 in / 20 cm), minimum tank (130 gal / 492 L), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), reef-safe status, diet emphasis on marine seaweed, and territoriality toward similar-shaped tangs.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), max size (23 cm TL), preferred temperature (24–28°C), depth range (1–20 m, typically 5–15 m), Indo-West Pacific range (Sulawesi to Philippines and Ryukyu Islands), habitat (clear lagoon and seaward reefs), and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature (72–79°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), reef-compatible, semi-aggressive temperament, and aggression toward other tangs. Minimum tank listed as 90 gal — lower than other sources but included in spread.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~20 cm / 8 in), minimum tank 125 gal, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, herbivorous algae-heavy diet, frequent small feedings, and 10–15 year lifespan in optimal conditions.

additional.name: Reef Chasers
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~20 cm), minimum 125 gal tank with 18+ inch width, susceptibility to ich and skin parasites, and aggression toward other tangs versus peaceful disposition toward non-tang tankmates.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found. Broadcast spawner in the wild; reported to hybridize with closely related Acanthurus species, notably A. nigricans.
